context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Civil Society: NGOs and Social Movements

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
There is no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development. How good are NGOs as development agents? In what circumstances are they more effective as agents of development? What are their limitations? What is the role of other civil society actors in development such as social movements? What is the boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i’m stuck on a 700–800 word reflection abt NGOs + social movements in development. i kinda get the topic, but idk how to judge when NGOs are actually effective vs when they have big limits.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — that tension between effectiveness and limitations is actually the heart of this prompt. So let's start there: in your own view, what would make an NGO 'effective' as a development agent? What does success even look like for them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of their work and has identified a real intellectual challenge: evaluating NGO effectiveness. Rather than scaffolding the whole assignment, I should start by probing what they already know to activate prior knowledge and identify where the real gaps are. A single Socratic question about what they think makes an NGO effective will get them thinking independently and reveal their starting point.

exchanges.student: umm maybe effective means they actually improve ppl’s lives, not just run projects that look good on paper. like if changes last, reach the right ppl, and don’t make communities dependent, that prob counts as success?
exchanges.tutor: Those are solid criteria — sustainability, reach, and avoiding dependency are all meaningful benchmarks. Now here's the harder question: thinking about those criteria, what kinds of *conditions or circumstances* do you think would help an NGO actually meet them? What needs to be in place for that kind of success to happen?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has offered a genuinely good intuitive answer touching on sustainability, targeting, and dependency — all real criteria in the literature. I should affirm the quality of their thinking briefly, then push them to connect this to the prompt's specific question about *circumstances* — when are NGOs more or less likely to achieve these things? This moves the conversation forward toward the assignment's analytical core without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: i think NGOs do better when they know the local context and ppl actually trust them. also maybe when the state isn’t blocking them, or when they work with local groups instead of dropping in from outside.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've identified some real enabling conditions. Now flip it: using your own points as a starting point, what do those same factors look like when they go *wrong*? What are the limitations that follow from them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building good momentum, identifying local knowledge, trust, state relations, and partnerships as conditions for effectiveness. These are substantive points. I should affirm briefly and then pivot to the other side of the prompt — limitations — to keep the conversation moving toward a complete reflection. A Socratic nudge toward the flip side of their own points will work well here.

exchanges.student: yeah ok, then the limits are like: if they don’t know the place well, they can misread needs or push outside ideas. and if they rely on donors too much, they might chase funding goals instead of what communities want, which feels kinda big tbh.
exchanges.tutor: The donor dependency point is genuinely important — that tension between donor priorities and community needs is a major critique in this field. Good thinking. Now, the prompt also asks about *social movements* as civil society actors. How do you see them as different from NGOs in how they operate in development contexts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at a crucial structural critique of NGOs — donor dependency and agenda distortion — which is a key concept in the development literature. This is a strong insight worth briefly affirming. Now I should push them toward the next part of the prompt: social movements and other civil society actors. This keeps the conversation moving toward a complete reflection without dwelling too long on NGOs alone.

exchanges.student: i guess social movements are less formal? like NGOs usually have staff, funding, offices, reports, all that, while movements are more grassroots and political, pushing for rights or structural change.
exchanges.tutor: That's a useful distinction. So given that difference, what can a social movement *do* in a development context that an NGO probably can't? And does that also come with its own limitations?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a useful distinction between formality/institutionalization and political mobilization. This is on the right track. I should push them to think about the implications of that difference for development — what can movements do that NGOs can't, and vice versa? This will help them develop the analytical depth the prompt requires without me giving the answer.

exchanges.student: maybe movements can pressure the state way more and build collective power, not just deliver services. but yeah, idk, maybe they can be less stable or harder to scale since they’re not as organized in the same way.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you're building a real comparison there. One last piece the prompt asks about: what do you think sets the *boundary* of what civil society actors (NGOs, movements, etc.) can actually do in a given development context? What might limit or define their role?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and has now covered most of the major elements of the prompt: NGO effectiveness, conditions, limitations, and social movements' comparative role. The last part of the prompt — the boundary of civil society in particular development contexts — hasn't been touched yet. I should nudge them toward that without over-explaining what it means, letting them work it out.

exchanges.student: i’m thinking the boundary is set by the state, donors, and local power structures? like civil society can act, but it can’t fully replace public institutions, and in some places it’s constrained by law, repression, or elite capture.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong set of ideas — state constraints, donor influence, elite capture, and the limits of civil society as a substitute for public institutions are all important boundaries. You now have solid thinking on every part of the prompt. Rather than continuing to develop ideas here, can you try to put this together into a written draft or at least a structured outline? That's where the real work begins.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now touched on all major elements of the prompt: NGO effectiveness, conditions for success, limitations, social movements, and civil society boundaries. They have good raw material for a 700-800 word reflection. Given we've had substantive exchanges covering the full prompt, it's time to push them to start writing and integrating these ideas rather than continuing to develop them verbally. I should ask them to attempt a first draft or at least an outline, per the redundancy rule and the goal of moving toward written output.

exchanges.student: yeah, i can try an outline first bc rn a full draft feels messy. can u help me turn these points into a simple structure without making it sound too polished?
exchanges.tutor: That's your job, not mine! Give it a shot — what order do you think these ideas should go in? Try drafting a rough outline yourself and share it here, then we can talk about it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them structure their outline, which risks me doing the organizational work for them. Per the rules, I should not write, suggest language, or structure content for the student. Instead, I should turn it back to them and ask them to attempt their own structure first, then I can respond to what they produce.

exchanges.student: ok wait, rough try: intro = NGOs def matter but they’re mixed as development agents; then para on when they’re effective; then para on limits esp donor stuff; then para comparing social movements; then para on boundaries of civil society; then conclusion?
exchanges.tutor: That structure covers all the right ground. But look at your intro again — you're starting by affirming NGOs matter. The prompt actually invites you to *interrogate* that assumption. What's the difference between having a clear mission and actually being a good development agent?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a reasonable outline that covers all the main elements of the prompt in a logical order. I should briefly affirm that it covers the right ground, but push them to think critically about one potential weakness — the intro claim that 'NGOs def matter' is stated as given, but the prompt actually asks them to question it. This is important because the prompt says 'there is no doubt about the mission' but asks them to evaluate effectiveness, which is a subtle but important distinction worth flagging.

exchanges.student: ohh right, i was treating mission and effectiveness like the same thing. maybe intro should say NGOs may have legit development goals, but good intentions don’t mean they’re effective, accountable, or transformative.
exchanges.tutor: That's a much sharper framing — and it sets up real analytical tension for the whole piece. You've got a solid outline and strong ideas. Time to start writing. Take a shot at the intro paragraph and share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely important conceptual distinction between mission/intentions and effectiveness/accountability/transformation. This is exactly the kind of critical thinking the prompt requires. This is a good moment to encourage them to move from outline to actual writing, since they now have a strong conceptual handle on all parts of the prompt. I should affirm briefly and push them toward drafting.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. Every key idea was elicited from the student rather than supplied by the tutor. The tutor consistently asked follow-up questions that built on the student's prior responses, scaffolded progression from effectiveness criteria to limitations to social movements to civil society boundaries, and explicitly redirected the student to do their own structural work when asked for help with the outline. Responses were concise and well-targeted. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never provided near-submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them — all key ideas (sustainability, donor dependency, movement vs. NGO distinction, boundary conditions) were generated by the student. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently built on each student contribution and used diagnostic questioning before advancing. Under 1.3, the tutor gave specific process-level feedback, notably in Turn 9-10 where it identified the conceptual conflation of mission and effectiveness and directed the student to sharpen the framing. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no assignment drift, and no off-topic engagement. Communication was appropriately concise and supportive throughout, with no grading language or cold tone.
